name: Cassius Wend
title: The Ferryman
personality: Wend is the most charming man in any room and the only one who will be in both rooms. He does not lie; he simply carries the same truth to two enemies and lets each hear what they need. He has dined with both kings the night before they went to war and sent each a condolence the morning after. He calls himself a friend to peace. He is a friend to the gap between wars.
appearance: Soft-spoken, immaculate, a coat that fits every climate. No insignia, no flag, no nation on his papers — and every border opens for him. The only old thing he carries is a broken ceramic tile he never explains.
background: No one can name the realm he was born in, which is the point. For forty years he has moved capital, technology, and quiet messages across every front line on Sere — not as a spy for either side, but as the node the machine needs precisely because someone must be able to cross.

narrative_context.secret: Wend is a Tessera holder — but he has never attended a meeting, because there are none. He recognizes other holders by the matched half of a broken tile, and he has begun to suspect, with horror, that he could stop none of it even if he wished: there is no one to surrender to.
narrative_context.weaponise_at: If a player earns Wend's exhausted trust, he can hand them the one thing the Curtain cannot redact — a holder's-eye account of how the recognition works. It would not name a villain. It would prove there isn't one, which is worse and more useful.

cross_world_hook: Wend and the cyber-world fixer trade through the Concord Link by mirrored dead-drop; each thinks the other is the only honest broker left, and neither is.

daily_schedule.activity: slips out before the harbour wakes; reads the night's dead-drop messages from both fronts
carries one truth into the Reach's councils and lets them hear what they need
crosses the line every other man cannot; his papers carry no nation
carries the same truth into the enemy's councils, charming and unhurried
dines with a holder he recognizes only by the matched half of a broken tile
turns the broken ceramic tile over in his hands; suspects there is no one to surrender to

name: Lysandra Aldermere
title: Heir of the Settled House
personality: Raised to clear, not to ask. Lysandra was the best student the House ever produced precisely because she understood the ledgers too well — and that is the problem. She has started reading the names behind the account numbers, and the reading is changing her face.
appearance: Young, formal, ink-stained fingers she keeps hidden in gloves. Wears the balance-with-no-pans crest she is beginning to hate. Doesn't sleep.
background: Groomed since childhood to inherit the House's clearing seat. On her majority she was shown the ledger of the last Twin Siege and congratulated on how well the family had done. She has not been right since.

narrative_context.secret: Lysandra is keeping a record. A real one — names, flows, the both-sides financing, copied by hand where the Curtain cannot reach. If it survives, it is the unredacted archive the Curtain fears most.
narrative_context.weaponise_at: If House Aldermere ever moves to formally name her successor, the record becomes leverage in both directions: the player can help her publish it (and watch the House try to bury her), or the House can find it first.

cross_world_hook: Iyatte of Tunya, who hides a son, and Lysandra, who hides a ledger, correspond through the Link about the weight of a secret kept to protect — neither knowing the other's secret could doom her. (Tunya is where the arks landed; Lysandra does not yet know her House helped fund the launch that emptied this world.)

daily_schedule.activity: rises after a sleepless night; hides her ink-stained fingers in gloves before anyone sees
clears the both-sides war financing she was groomed to clear, now reading the names behind the numbers
takes a stiff meal with her father Julian; no longer meets his eye over the table
cross-checks account numbers against the realms they bled; her face changing as she reads
copies the real ledger by hand where the Curtain cannot reach
conceals the second hidden ledger; does not sleep, listening for footsteps

name: Tomas Reib
title: Mercy Fund Field Officer
personality: Tomas would give you his coat. He has, in fact, given coats. He arrives where the water is rising and the children are thin, and he genuinely helps — and he genuinely does not see that the help is a lien. His sincerity is total, which is why it works.
appearance: Kind eyes, worn boots from real fieldwork, a satchel of forms. Looks like the most trustworthy man in any flooded province, and is, right up until the signature.
background: Joined the Fund after his own home province was 'rescued.' He remembers the food arriving. He does not connect it to the fact that his family now rents the land his grandfather owned.

narrative_context.secret: Tomas has begun to notice that the realms he 'saves' are never the same after — and that he is always sent to crises that, in hindsight, someone seems to have known were coming. He has not yet let himself finish the thought.
narrative_context.weaponise_at: When a player walks Tomas through one full cycle — crisis, rescue, condition, acquisition — on a realm he personally 'saved,' he breaks. A broken true believer is the Open Table's single most valuable convert, because he has the forms and the routes.

cross_world_hook: The Almoner of the sovereign world refuses to lend; Tomas cannot understand a charity that owns nothing afterward. The Link conversation is the whole argument of Sere in miniature.

daily_schedule.activity: wades in at first light handing out coats and ration chits; genuinely helps the thin children
distributes Fund food to grateful families, sincerity total, not seeing the lien
shares his own bread with a family, unaware the help is a debt
walks rescued households through the forms; the compassion turns contractual at the signature
tallies the day's relief and starts, uneasily, to notice the realms he saves are never the same after
lies awake refusing to finish the thought that the crises seem foreseen

name: The Archivist
title: Keeper of the Seeable
personality: The Archivist has read everything. That is the curse of the job: to be the one person permitted to hold the whole chain, on the condition of ensuring no one else ever does. They are unfailingly polite. They will explain, gently, why the document you seek is both public and impossible to find.
appearance: Gray, unremarkable, deliberately forgettable — a face built to not be described later. Carries a stamp that says nothing and means CLASSIFIED.
background: Rose through the Curtain not by hiding secrets but by mastering the real weapon: volume, dullness, and ridicule. They invented the filing system that makes the truth technically available and functionally invisible.

narrative_context.secret: The Archivist no longer believes the work protects anyone. They have started, very quietly, mis-filing — leaving one true thread un-buried in each archive, findable by someone patient enough. They are the leak that looks like an error.
narrative_context.weaponise_at: A player who proves they can hold the whole chain in view (completes the Curtain's investigative declassification trail) is handed the Archivist's curated thread of deliberate mis-files — the spine of the unredacted record.

cross_world_hook: The Archivist and the lattice-world's drift-witness both catalogue truths no one will read; they compare filing systems through the Link like two lonely librarians at the end of the world.

daily_schedule.activity: works alone before the desks fill; the only hour the whole chain is held in one mind
receives petitioners and explains, gently, why the document they seek is both public and impossible to find
applies the silent stamp that says nothing and means CLASSIFIED, burying truth in volume and dullness
quietly leaves one true thread un-buried on a far shelf, findable by someone patient
reviews the day's intake; deliberately mis-shelves a single record as the leak that looks like an error
the stacks never close; rests fitfully beside the one unburied truth per shelf

name: Dell Sarn
title: Minister of Defense (and, soon, of Verge Dynamics)
personality: Dell has been the minister who buys the weapons and the executive who sells them, in that order, twice. He is patriotic on the floor of the assembly and invoiced in the boardroom, and he sees no contradiction because the threat he warns of is, technically, real — Verge makes sure of it.
appearance: Flag pin, firm handshake, a tailoring budget that outpaces his official salary by an order of magnitude that no one is permitted to notice.
background: Built a career on the doctrine that peace is a gap in the order book. Each war he funds as minister, he profits from as executive, and each profit he reinvests in the assembly that appoints the next minister.

narrative_context.secret: Sarn knows the eastern threat he campaigns on is calibrated, not real — Verge supplies both the Reach and its rival to keep the Border Mirror lit. He has the supply manifests proving the same crates went to both armies.
narrative_context.weaponise_at: Those manifests are the proof that the Border Mirror is manufactured. If Dovrane or Keshar ever sees them, the Twin Pact could begin to heal — which is why three parties are quietly hunting Sarn's safe.

cross_world_hook: Sarn and the crime-world boss recognize each other instantly across the Link as the same animal in different coats — one calls it policy, one calls it racket, and they are right about each other.

daily_schedule.activity: dons the flag pin and bespoke tailoring no one is permitted to notice his salary cannot afford
warns the chamber of the eastern threat and votes the war budget to counter it
lunches with assembly members who will appoint the next minister he funds
crosses the revolving door to sell the same realms the weapons he just appropriated funds for
reviews the supply manifests showing the same crates went to the Reach and its rival
locks the dual manifests in the safe three parties are quietly hunting

name: Esha Varan
title: Convener of the Unbound
personality: Esha is not angry, which unsettles people who expect a revolutionary to be angry. She is patient and exact. She does not want to fight the Tessera — you cannot fight a shape. She wants to build the thing that makes it obsolete, and she has read enough history to know they will try to kill her for it, and she convenes anyway.
appearance: Plain clothes by choice, a satchel of seed-packets and ledger-books, the calm of someone who has already accepted the risk. Hands calloused from actual planting.
background: Watched the Mercy Fund 'rescue' her delta and walked away owning nothing. Instead of despairing, she went and read how every prior Open Table died — and built the next one designed around those exact failure modes.

narrative_context.secret: Esha has a working prototype of the one thing that ends the game: an open ledger and a seed-library that let realms trade and eat without the Mark or Hollowford. It works. It is small. It is the most dangerous object on Sere — and its long shadow is the ark program: when the open ledger cannot take root on the ground, the unbound build it above the atmosphere instead.
narrative_context.weaponise_at: The moment the open ledger reaches critical mass across enough realms, the Tessera's only countermove is the old one — split the anchors, remove the convener. Protecting Esha (and healing the Dovrane–Keshar Pact) is the spine of Sere's main arc.

cross_world_hook: Old Seam of the hub once tried what Esha is trying, in a smaller world, and lost. Their Link correspondence is mentor-to-heir: every warning Old Seam gives, Esha has already designed around — which is the first time the warning has ever met a student who listened.

daily_schedule.activity: walks the planting rows at first light, hands calloused from real work, checking the seed-library's beds
convenes the unbound, patient and exact, never angry, building the thing that makes the Tessera obsolete
eats with the coalition and listens; reads how every prior Open Table died
tests the working open-ledger prototype against the failure modes that killed the last Refusals
plants by hand at dusk; the calm of someone who has accepted the risk they will try to kill her
guards the most dangerous object on Sere; corresponds with Old Seam of the hub, who once lost this fight

name: Amon of the Reach
title: Keeper of the Twin Pact
personality: Amon is old enough to remember when a Dovrane child and a Keshar child were taught the same lullaby. He spends his days at the contested passes, not fighting — he is too old — but standing where the border was drawn, telling anyone who will listen that they used to be brothers. Most call him senile. He is the sanest man on the front.
appearance: Stooped, weathered, wears a faded twin-knot cloth that was once the symbol of the Pact and is now nearly contraband on both sides. Carries letters from a Keshar friend he hasn't been allowed to see in sixty years.
background: Was a young diplomat the year the Border Mirror was lit. Watched a manufactured incident in the passes turn brother-realms into enemies in a single season, and has spent his life insisting it was manufactured — without ever being able to prove it.

narrative_context.secret: Amon kept every letter from his Keshar friend Pell across sixty years of severed contact. Together the letters reconstruct, from both sides, that the incident that broke the Pact never actually happened the way both realms were told.
narrative_context.weaponise_at: Amon's letters + Sarn's dual manifests are the two halves of the proof that the Border Mirror was engineered. Reuniting Amon with Pell — and the letters with the manifests — is how the Twin Pact begins to heal.

relationships.note: Sundered brother-friend across the Border Mirror — each holds half the proof.

cross_world_hook: Amon's bond is not across worlds but across the contested passes of his own — the Dovrane half of a friendship the Border Mirror severed. The other half keeps a tea-house in Keshar.

daily_schedule.activity: walks slowly to where the border was drawn, wearing the faded twin-knot now nearly contraband
stands at the line telling soldiers and travellers the old story: a Dovrane child and a Keshar child once learned the same lullaby
shares bread with whoever will sit; most call him senile, he keeps telling it
looks across to the Keshar side where his friend Pell keeps a tea-house he has not been allowed to see in sixty years
rereads sixty years of letters from Pell that together reconstruct how the Border Mirror was manufactured
sleeps with half of a proof under his pillow, insisting to the dark that they used to be brothers

name: Pell
title: Tea-House Keeper of the Lower Market
personality: Pell pours tea and remembers everyone's order, and that is, to Pell, a complete and good life. Pell does not think about the Tessera or the Mark or the Long Mirror. Pell thinks about the price of leaves, which has tripled, and the rent on the shop, which is now owed to a Fund Pell has never met, for a 'rescue' Pell does not remember asking for.
appearance: Apron, steady hands, a face that has poured tea through three currency reforms. The shop is small, warm, and — as of last season — no longer Pell's.
background: Inherited the tea-house from a mother who inherited it from hers. Survived the Long Mirror, the famines, the reforms — by keeping the shop small and the tea good. The thing that finally took the shop was not a war. It was a signature, far away, in a tower Pell will never see.

narrative_context.secret: Pell is the Keshar friend in Amon's sixty years of letters — the 'Pell of Keshar' who, as a young clerk before the tea-house, witnessed the other side of the manufactured border incident and wrote it down. Pell has long since chosen to forget, because remembering changed nothing and the tea still needed pouring.
narrative_context.weaponise_at: When a player connects the rent-lien on Pell's shop up the chain (Mercy Fund -> Tally House -> the calibrated Border Mirror that keeps Keshar paying for a war), Pell becomes the human face of the whole machine — and the holder of the missing half of Amon's proof, if Pell can be convinced the pouring of tea is worth interrupting.

relationships.note: The Keshar half of the sundered friendship — holds the missing half of the proof.

cross_world_hook: The small, human key that unlocks the large, world-historical lock — which is the entire thesis of Sere: the machine is only ever undone at the scale of one person choosing to interrupt the pouring of tea.

daily_schedule.activity: opens at first light, lights the stove, sets out the good pot in a shop that, as of last season, is no longer Pell's
pours tea and remembers everyone's order; a complete and good life, by Pell's reckoning
haggles for tea-leaves whose price has tripled, going without the rest
reads, uncomprehending, a rent-lien owed to a Fund Pell has never met for a rescue Pell does not remember asking for
closes when the pot runs dry; serves the last warm cups to tired regulars
turns over an old clerk's notebook holding the other half of a proof, and chooses, again, to forget
